title: Whether to put the night-soil men in the museum
summary: Critique a city museum's plan to honour the men who once carried away its sewage by night — asking whether a display dignifies a despised trade or merely tidies a humiliation into something the powerful can admire at a safe distance.
topic: The moral weight of commemorating a forgotten and degrading trade
prompt: Write a 600–800-word critical essay assessing a museum's plan to commemorate a vanished, low-status trade (e.g. night-soil collection, tanning, gut-cleaning). Ground it in one concrete trade and city. Examine the paradox that recognition can also aestheticise and defuse a real injustice, and resist a clean verdict. Use hedging, nominalization, and at least one complex concession.
hintStarters: On the wall, beside a lacquered wooden bucket, the panel called the work 'essential'; what it did not say was that…
It is tempting to read the new gallery as overdue recognition, yet the gesture is harder to trust once…
If commemoration can repair a slight, it can also perform one, and the line between the two runs through…

sampleText: On the wall of the city's new heritage gallery, beside a lacquered wooden bucket and a yoke worn smooth by shoulders long dead, the panel called the work 'essential to the health of old Seoul'. What it did not say, in so many words, was that the men who emptied the city's privies before dawn were paid in scraps, lived apart, and married only among themselves, because no one else would have them. I stood there for a while, genuinely moved, and then less sure of what I was being moved by. It is tempting to read the gallery as overdue recognition. The gesture is harder to trust once you ask who, exactly, is now being thanked, and by whom, and for what. The night-soil carriers are not in the room. Their descendants, where they can be found, were not consulted on the wording. The people doing the thanking are, by and large, the heirs of the people who once crossed the street to avoid the smell. There is something uneasy in watching a class apologise to a class it has outlived, in a building it paid for, on terms it set. None of this makes the commemoration worthless. A trade that held a city together and was repaid with contempt has, if anything is owed to anyone, some claim on the record. The difficulty is that recognition is rarely a single, clean act. It can repair a slight; it can also perform one. To aestheticise the bucket — to light it well, to call the yoke 'beautifully made' — risks turning a humiliation into a craft object, something the comfortable can admire at a safe distance precisely because the discomfort has been drained out of it. The smell is gone. So, almost, is the shame, and with the shame goes the part that ought to implicate the visitor. What survives is a feeling of having paid one's respects, which is not at all the same as having understood what the respect is for. I do not want to argue that the museum should therefore have left the men out, dressing the past in the same silence that disgraced them while they lived. A history that records only the trades it finds tasteful is not more honest; it is merely better-mannered. The men did the work; the work was real; the contempt was real. To leave all of it unspoken in the name of delicacy would simply continue the original injury by quieter means. Yet the case suggests something the heritage industry is reluctant to say aloud: that the act of honouring the lowly is also, almost always, flattering to the one doing the honouring. The visitor leaves ennobled by association, a little more reconciled to a city whose order was built, in part, on someone else's permanent indignity. The discomfort that might have done some moral work has been converted into the warm, harmless glow of having cared. What would a more truthful panel have said? Not, I think, a confession bolted to the wall, which is its own kind of performance. Something plainer. It could have named the wages. It could have said that the men were shunned, and by whom, and that the shunning long outlasted the trade. It could have admitted that the comfort of the modern visitor rests on a sanitation those men carried, in buckets, on their backs, while being treated as if they were the filth they removed. None of this would resolve the unease; nothing resolves it. The dead cannot be paid, and an apology arriving a century late, addressed to no one in particular, is at best a gesture toward a debt that can no longer be settled. But there is a difference between commemoration that helps the living feel they have squared an account and commemoration that admits the account remains open. The first ennobles the trade and quietly excuses the city. The second keeps the discomfort where it belongs. The least a museum can do, having decided to remember the men at last, is to remember them in a way that does not let the rest of us off so lightly.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Semicolon-balanced antithesis for argued contrast
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Tác giả dùng dấu chấm phẩy để dựng các cặp đối lập cân xứng: 'It can repair a slight; it can also perform one' và 'is not more honest; it is merely better-mannered'. Cấu trúc song song hai vế ngắn, ngắt bằng dấu chấm phẩy, nén một sự nhượng bộ và một phản bác vào cùng một nhịp — dấu ấn của lập luận C1 chặt chẽ.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: The writer uses the semicolon to mount balanced antitheses: 'It can repair a slight; it can also perform one' and 'is not more honest; it is merely better-mannered'. Pairing two short parallel clauses across a semicolon compresses a concession and a counter-claim into one beat — a marker of tight C1 argument.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Nominalization to make a process the subject
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Các danh hoá như 'recognition', 'commemoration', 'the shunning' và 'the act of honouring' biến hành động thành chủ thể có thể gán hậu quả: 'recognition is rarely a single, clean act'. Nhờ đó tác giả lập luận về bản thân quá trình chứ không chỉ về người thực hiện — đặc trưng văn phong học thuật C1.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: Nominalizations such as 'recognition', 'commemoration', and 'the shunning' turn actions into subjects that can carry consequence: 'recognition is rarely a single, clean act'. This lets the writer argue about the process itself, not merely the people doing it — a hallmark of C1 academic register.

sampleTranslationVi: Trên bức tường của phòng trưng bày di sản mới của thành phố, cạnh một chiếc thùng gỗ sơn mài và một cái đòn gánh mòn nhẵn bởi những bờ vai đã chết từ lâu, tấm bảng gọi công việc ấy là 'thiết yếu với sức khoẻ của Seoul xưa'. Điều nó không nói thẳng ra là những người đàn ông dọn sạch nhà xí của thành phố trước rạng đông được trả bằng đồ thừa, sống tách biệt, và chỉ lấy nhau trong cùng tầng lớp, vì chẳng ai khác chịu nhận họ. Tôi đứng đó một lúc, thật lòng xúc động, rồi bớt chắc chắn về điều gì đang khiến mình xúc động. Thật dễ để đọc phòng trưng bày này như một sự ghi nhận muộn màng. Nhưng cử chỉ ấy trở nên khó tin hơn khi ta hỏi rốt cuộc ai đang được cảm ơn, và bởi ai, và vì cái gì. Những người gánh phân không có mặt trong căn phòng. Con cháu họ, ở nơi còn tìm được, không được hỏi ý về cách dùng từ. Những người đang đi cảm ơn, nói chung, là con cháu của những kẻ từng băng qua đường để tránh cái mùi. Có điều gì đó bất an khi chứng kiến một giai tầng xin lỗi một giai tầng mà nó đã sống lâu hơn, trong một toà nhà do nó bỏ tiền, theo những điều khoản do nó đặt ra. Không điều nào trong số đó khiến sự tưởng niệm trở nên vô giá trị. Một cái nghề từng giữ cho cả thành phố vận hành mà chỉ được đáp lại bằng khinh miệt, nếu có gì nợ ai đó, thì cũng có ít nhiều quyền được ghi vào lịch sử. Cái khó là sự ghi nhận hiếm khi là một hành động đơn nhất, sạch sẽ. Nó có thể sửa chữa một sự coi thường; nó cũng có thể tự gây ra một sự coi thường. Thẩm mỹ hoá chiếc thùng — chiếu sáng thật khéo, gọi cái đòn gánh là 'làm đẹp lắm' — có nguy cơ biến một sự hạ nhục thành một món đồ thủ công, thứ mà những kẻ sung túc có thể ngắm nghía từ khoảng cách an toàn chính vì sự khó chịu đã bị rút cạn khỏi nó. Cái mùi đã biến mất. Sự xấu hổ cũng gần như vậy, và cùng với sự xấu hổ là cái phần lẽ ra phải khiến người xem thấy mình có liên đới. Cái còn lại là cảm giác đã tỏ lòng kính trọng, vốn hoàn toàn không giống với việc đã hiểu sự kính trọng ấy là vì điều gì. Tôi không muốn lập luận rằng vì thế bảo tàng lẽ ra nên bỏ những người này ra ngoài, khoác lên quá khứ chính sự im lặng đã làm nhục họ khi còn sống. Một lịch sử chỉ ghi lại những nghề mà nó thấy thanh nhã không hề trung thực hơn; nó chỉ lịch sự hơn mà thôi. Những người ấy đã làm việc; công việc là có thật; sự khinh miệt là có thật. Để tất cả những điều đó không được nói ra nhân danh sự tế nhị chỉ là tiếp nối sự tổn thương ban đầu bằng những cách lặng lẽ hơn. Thế nhưng trường hợp này gợi ra điều mà ngành di sản ngại nói thành lời: rằng việc vinh danh kẻ thấp hèn cũng gần như luôn là sự tâng bốc chính người đi vinh danh. Người xem ra về được nâng tầm cao quý nhờ liên đới, hoà giải hơn một chút với một thành phố mà trật tự của nó được dựng nên, một phần, trên nỗi nhục lâu dài của người khác. Sự khó chịu lẽ ra có thể làm nên chút việc đạo đức nào đó đã bị chuyển hoá thành thứ ánh sáng ấm áp, vô hại của cảm giác đã từng quan tâm. Một tấm bảng trung thực hơn lẽ ra sẽ viết gì? Tôi nghĩ, không phải một lời thú tội đóng đinh lên tường, vốn là một kiểu trình diễn của riêng nó. Một điều gì đó mộc mạc hơn. Nó có thể nêu ra mức lương. Nó có thể nói rằng những người ấy bị xa lánh, và bởi ai, và rằng sự xa lánh đó tồn tại lâu hơn cả cái nghề. Nó có thể thừa nhận rằng sự tiện nghi của người xem thời nay tựa trên một thứ vệ sinh mà những người ấy đã gánh, trong những chiếc thùng, trên lưng họ, trong khi bị đối xử như thể chính họ là thứ dơ bẩn mà họ dọn đi. Không điều nào trong số đó xoá được nỗi bất an; chẳng có gì xoá được nó cả. Người chết thì không thể được trả công, và một lời xin lỗi đến muộn cả thế kỷ, gửi cho không một ai cụ thể, cùng lắm cũng chỉ là một cử chỉ hướng về một món nợ không còn có thể trả được nữa. Nhưng có một khác biệt giữa sự tưởng niệm giúp người sống cảm thấy mình đã trang trải xong một món nợ và sự tưởng niệm thừa nhận rằng món nợ vẫn còn bỏ ngỏ. Cái thứ nhất nâng tầm cái nghề và lặng lẽ bào chữa cho thành phố. Cái thứ hai giữ sự khó chịu ở đúng chỗ của nó. Điều tối thiểu một bảo tàng có thể làm, một khi đã quyết định cuối cùng cũng tưởng nhớ những người ấy, là tưởng nhớ họ theo một cách không tha bổng cho phần còn lại của chúng ta một cách dễ dàng như vậy.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence best captures the essay's central paradox about commemoration?
options: Commemoration is always good because it remembers forgotten people.
Museums should never display low-status trades.
Recognition is rarely a single, clean act: it can repair a slight, but it can also perform one.
There are pros and cons to building heritage galleries.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B states the paradox the essay turns on; A and C are one-sided, and D is empty 'fake balance' filler.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the line that says recognition can both fix AND inflict a slight.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay mean by saying the discomfort 'has been drained out of it'?
options: The bucket was literally cleaned for display.
Visitors found the exhibit boring.
The museum hid the bucket from view.
By presenting the trade as a tasteful craft object, the exhibit removes the moral unease that ought to implicate the visitor.
mcQuestions.explanation: The metaphor describes aestheticising the work so the visitor can admire it without feeling implicated.
mcQuestions.hint: Connect it to 'aestheticise' and 'admire at a safe distance'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Why does the essay reject leaving the men out of the museum?
options: Because a history that records only tasteful trades is not more honest, merely better-mannered, and silence would continue the original injury.
Because the museum needs more exhibits to attract visitors.
Because the descendants demanded a gallery.
Because the buckets are valuable antiques.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay's concession: omission would just continue the disgrace 'by quieter means'.
mcQuestions.hint: Find where 'better-mannered' is contrasted with 'more honest'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option is a nominalization, the C1 feature flagged in the grammar notes?
options: to gloss over
the shunning
well-meaning
at arm's length
mcQuestions.explanation: 'The shunning' turns the verb 'shun' into a noun that can be the subject of a claim — a defining C1 nominalization.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for a noun made from a verb that names a process.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence best demonstrates the complex concession the prompt requires?
options: Museums are dishonest, so they should close.
There are good and bad sides to commemorating dirty work.

I do not want to argue the museum should have left the men out … Yet the case suggests something the heritage industry is reluctant to say aloud: that honouring the lowly also flatters the one doing the honouring.
The men did dirty work and were poor.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C fully grants the opposing view, then uses 'Yet…' to introduce a narrowed, qualified counter-claim — a complex C1 concession.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for a full grant of the objection followed by a restricting 'Yet…'.

options: The night-soil men being given noble titles.
The museum being awarded a prize.
The bucket being made of precious material.
The modern visitor feeling morally elevated simply by sympathising with the trade.
mcQuestions.explanation: The phrase names the self-flattery the essay critiques: the visitor leaves feeling good for having 'cared'.
mcQuestions.hint: Who gains the noble feeling — the dead, or the visitor?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses the best cohesion to follow 'The smell is gone.'?
options: So, almost, is the shame, and with the shame goes the part that ought to implicate the visitor.
Also, the gallery has a coffee shop near the entrance.
Smell is detected by receptors in the nose.
In today's museums, smells are carefully controlled.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B uses ellipsis and parallel structure ('So, almost, is the shame') to extend the thought; the others are off-topic, definitional, or filler.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option continues the same idea and links 'smell' to 'shame'?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which paraphrase is closest to 'commemoration that admits the account remains open'?
options: Commemoration that proves the debt has finally been paid.
A form of remembering that acknowledges the injustice has not been, and perhaps cannot be, settled.
Commemoration that opens a new financial account for the museum.
Remembering that lets the city move on with a clear conscience.
mcQuestions.explanation: The 'open account' is a metaphor for an unsettled debt; option B captures that, while A and D invert it.
mcQuestions.hint: An 'open account' is unpaid — which paraphrase keeps the debt unpaid?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence is grammatical, on-topic, and matches the essay's reflective register?
options: The museum totally nailed it by finally giving the toilet guys some love.
It is an undeniable fact that all museums whitewash history without exception.
I stood there for a while, genuinely moved, and then less sure of what I was being moved by.
Pursuant to heritage guidelines, the artefact's significance was duly documented.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B is reflective and precise; A is slang, C is a sweeping absolute, D is bureaucratic for a personal essay.
mcQuestions.hint: Match thoughtful first-person prose — not slang, legalese, or absolutism.

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the rhetorical effect of 'treated as if they were the filth they removed'?
options: It literally claims the men were made of waste.
It praises the men's resistance to disease.
It explains the chemistry of night soil.
It collapses worker and waste into one image to expose how the stigma erased the distinction between the person and the dirty task.
mcQuestions.explanation: The image fuses the men with what they carried, dramatising the dehumanising stigma.
mcQuestions.hint: Why fuse the worker with the substance they removed?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which word best fills the gap in C1 register? 'To ___ the bucket — to light it well — risks turning a humiliation into a craft object.'
options: aestheticise
clean up
look at
show off
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Aestheticise' (turn into something to be admired as beautiful) is the precise term; the others are too casual or imprecise.
mcQuestions.hint: Which verb means 'present as an object of beauty'?

mcQuestions.prompt: Why does the essay distinguish 'feeling they have squared an account' from facing an 'open' one?
options: To argue the museum should charge a higher entrance fee.
To separate commemoration that lets the living feel absolved from commemoration that keeps the injustice acknowledged.
To recommend keeping financial records of all exhibits.
To prove the descendants were owed money.
mcQuestions.explanation: The distinction is between false closure ('squared') and honest acknowledgement ('open').
mcQuestions.hint: Which version lets people off, and which doesn't?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option preserves the hedged tone the prompt requires?
options: Every museum erases the poor on purpose.
It is obvious the curators wanted to feel good about themselves.
Honouring the lowly is also, almost always, flattering to the one doing the honouring.
No exhibit has ever told the truth about labour.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Also, almost always' hedges the generalisation; the other options are absolute or accusatory.
mcQuestions.hint: Hedging softens a claim — look for qualifying adverbs.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay propose a 'more truthful panel' would do?
options: Bolt a grand public confession to the wall.
Remove the bucket entirely.
Let visitors vote on whether the trade was honourable.
State plainer facts — the wages, the shunning, who shunned them — without dramatising guilt.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay calls for 'something plainer' — naming wages and stigma — not a performed confession.
mcQuestions.hint: The essay contrasts a 'confession bolted to the wall' with 'something plainer'.

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the function of the final sentence, about not letting 'the rest of us off so lightly'?
options: It accepts that remembering is right while insisting the remembering keep the visitor implicated rather than absolved.
It offers a full solution that settles the historical debt.
It blames individual curators for the whole stigma.
It recommends the museum stop displaying the trade.
mcQuestions.explanation: The close holds the paradox open: remember the men, but in a way that resists comfortable absolution.
mcQuestions.hint: The essay said nothing resolves the unease — so what modest demand does it end on?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option best paraphrases 'a class apologise to a class it has outlived'?
options: Two social groups apologising to each other equally.
The descendants of the privileged thanking a group that has effectively vanished, on terms the privileged still control.
Old people apologising to young people.
A museum apologising to its donors.
mcQuestions.explanation: The phrase captures an uneven, one-sided apology delivered after the wronged group is gone.
mcQuestions.hint: Who is gone, and who sets the terms?

criticalThinkingQuestion: Think of a job in your own society that people quietly look down on but could not live without. If it were honoured in a museum a hundred years from now, what would an honest panel have to admit about how it is treated today — and would you want to read it?
